learning_outcomes.description: Articulate the logic and benefits of advanced quantitative social science research
Interpret advanced quantitative analyses in published academic and non-academic research outputs
Review and critique advanced quantitative analyses done by others
Identify the appropriate regression model for analysing continuous or categorical data
Appreciate conceptual differences between confounding, moderation and mediation
Use statistical software to access, manage and manipulate survey data in preparation for quantitative analyses
Use statistical software to undertake bivariate and multiple linear and logistic regression models
Access and manage data according to the principles of responsible data use
Understand core concepts and tools for causal inference
Gain familiarity with advanced study designs for multilevel, longitudinal, and experimental/quasi-experimental research.

assessments.due_date:
8/09/2026 11:00 am

This exam will be completed during class time.
assessments.task_description:
This is multiple and short response exam that will assess student's understanding of basic statistical concepts.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submissions will be collected in class by the lecturer.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
A computer and statistical assignment worth 35% of the overall course grade. This assignment will be placed on Blackboard in Week 6 and will be completed in a three-week period (weeks 7, 8, and 9). The assignment will pose a series of separate questions that with cover the material covered in the lectures and lab sessions. There is no strict word limit for this assignment, but it is expected that students' combined responses will be no longer than 1,000 words (excluding software output, tables, graphs, and log files).

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:

This assignment will be placed on Blackboard in Week 10 and will be completed in a three-week period (weeks 10, 11, and 12). There is no strict word limit for this assignment, but it is expected that students' combined responses will be no longer than 1,000 words (excluding Stata output, tables, graphs, and Stata log files).

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

learning_activities.topic:
Course introduction and getting started with R (Week 1)

This lecture will introduce the course. Among others, it will cover the course structure, the objectives, the recommended readings, and the assessment.


Learning outcomes: L01, L06, L08
learning_activities.topic_title: Course introduction and getting started with R (Week 1)
learning_activities.topic_description: Course introduction and getting started with R (Week 1) This lecture will introduce the course. Among others, it will cover the course structure, the objectives, the recommended readings, and the assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Revision of statistical concepts and R basics (Week 2)

This lecture will provide a revision of key ideas concerning the logic of quantitative research, the properties of quantitative data, bivariate data analyses, and foundational notions in statistical inference. This is a refresher on/summary of material covered in 'SOCY2339 Introducing Quantitative Research'.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L06, L08
